entry.title: Manifold and Riemannian Geometry (X+1): Connections, Parallel Transport, and Geodesics

link.title: Manifold and Riemannian Geometry (X+1): Connections, Parallel Transport, and Geodesics

entry.content:
This is episode X on the Manifold and Riemannian Geometry series. Today we will be exploring more on tangent vectors, vector fields, another key concept about how to relate tangent spaces together, and many other derivative notions crucial for the development of Riemannian Geometry.

0] Intuition and Introduction: Directional Derivatives

Connection: Bridging derivatives from \(\mathbb{R}^n\) to curved manifolds. The concept of a connection is the necessary tool that allows us to perform differential calculus on curved spaces (manifolds), such as the surface of a sphere. It generalizes the familiar idea of the directional derivative from flat Euclidean space (\(\mathbb{R}^n\)).

Directional Derivatives in Euclidean Space (\(\mathbb{R}^3\)) In \(\mathbb{R}^3\) with Cartesian coordinates \((x, y, z)\), the directional derivative provides a simple way to measure change. The basis vectors \(\left\{ \mathbf{i}, \mathbf{j}, \mathbf{k} ight\}\) (or the equivalent operators \(\left\{ rac{\partial}{\partial x}, rac{\partial}{\partial y}, rac{\partial}{\partial z} ight\}\)) are constant, allowing us to define derivatives simply as component-wise partial derivatives. Let \(p=(1, 2, 0)\) be a point, \(X=(y, -x, 3x)\) be the direction vector field, and \(V=(xz, y^2, -2x)\) be a vector field.

There are two things we could implement:

1) Derivative of a Scalar Function (\(D_X f\)).

This is the directional derivative of a smooth scalar function \(f\) in the direction \(X\).

For example, for \(f(x,y,z)=xy^2+z\) at \(p\): By definition, \(D_X f = abla f \cdot X\), so \(D_X f(p) = \langle 4, 4, 1 angle \cdot \langle 2, -1, 3 angle = \mathbf{7}\)

Point Derivation (Geometry) \(X[f] = X^i rac{\partial f}{\partial x^i}\) \(Xf = (y^3 - 2x^2y + 3x)\)

This confirms that in differential geometry, a tangent vector \(X\) is rigorously defined as a point derivation: an operator that mimics the directional derivative by satisfying the Leibniz rule (please see my previous blog on the definition and interpretation of tangent vectors).

2) Derivative of a Vector Field (\(D_X V\))

This derivative measures how the vector field \(V\) changes as we move in the direction \(X\). In \(\mathbb{R}^3\), this is calculated by taking the directional derivative of each component of \(V\).

Using the vector fields \(X\) and \(V\), we notice that the \(k\)-th component of the resulting vector \(D_X V\) is \((D_X V)^k = \sum_{i} X^i rac{\partial V^k}{\partial x^i}\).

Component 1 (i.e., \(k=1\)): \((D_X V)^1 = yz + 3x^2\) Component 2 (i.e., \(k=2\)): \((D_X V)^2 = -2xy\) Component 3 (i.e., \(k=3\)): \((D_X V)^3 = -2y\)

Evaluating at \(p=(1, 2, 0)\) gives:

\[D_X V(p) = \langle 3, -4, -4 angle\]

1] Why Do We Need a Connection?

In Euclidean space \(\mathbb{R}^{n}\), if we have a vector field \(Y(x) = (Y^1(x), \cdots, Y^n(x))\), and we could easily differentiate component-wise: \( rac{\partial Y^i}{\partial x^j}\). This works because

  • All tangent spaces are the same vector space.
  • We can subtract vectors at different points.
  • We can differentiate vector fields componentwise.

But on a manifold:

  • A vector field satisfies
    \(V(p) \in T_pM\) \(V(q) \in T_qM\)
  • These live in different vector spaces.
  • Subtracting them directly makes no sense.

So we need a rule that tells us:

How does a vector field \(Y\) change as we move in a given direction given by \(X\)?

This rule is an affine connection. And we choose such a rule.

2] Definition of an Affine Connection

A connection is an operator operator \( abla: C^{\infty}(M) imes C^{\infty}(M) o C^{\infty}(M)\) that maps two vector fields, \(X\) and \(Y\), to a new vector field \( abla_X Y\).

We could think of it as:

The derivative of \(Y\) in the direction \(X\).

This specified choice needs to satisfy the following three rules:

  • Linearity in \(X\) \( abla_{fX + gZ} Y = f abla_X Y + g abla_Z Y\)

  • Linearity in \(Y\) \( abla_X (Y + Z) = abla_X Y + abla_X Z\)

  • Leibniz Rule \( abla_X (fY) = X(f)Y + f abla_X Y\)

where $X(f) = Xf$ is the directional derivative of \(f\). This last property is crucial, as it mirrors ordinary differentiation (notice that we also require the definition of derivation when in tangent vector to also satisfy the Leibniz rule).

Also note that this definition does not require a metric.

3] Local Coordinate Formula and Christoffel Symbols

Choose coordinates \((x^1, \dots, x^n)\) first.

Let
\(X_i = rac{\partial}{\partial x^i}\)

Note that for the following I might interchange these two symbols, but they are always equivalent.

Define:

\[ abla_{X_i} X_j = \Gamma^k_{ij} X_k\]

(Or, just in another format, to be crystal clear)

\[ abla_{ rac{\partial}{\partial x_i}} rac{\partial}{\partial x_j} = \sum_{k = 1}^n \Gamma^k_{ij} rac{\partial}{\partial x_k}\]

We use above the Einstein summation notation as convention (to simplify notations). In below, whenever summation might be obvious I will drop it. Here the $\Gamma^k_{ij}$ are called the Christoffel symbols, which tell us how the coordinate basis vectors change as we move.

Now use the above notation, say for two vector fields:

\(X = X^i rac{\partial}{\partial x^i}\) \(Y = Y^j rac{\partial}{\partial x^j}\)

We could obtain:

\[ oxed{ abla_X Y = \left(\underbrace{ X^i rac{\partial Y^k}{\partial x^i} }_{ ext{I. Flat-Space Derivative Term}} + \underbrace{X^i \Gamma^k_{ij} Y^j }_{ ext{II. Curvature Correction Term}} ight) rac{\partial}{\partial{x^k}} }\]

This will be our main formula. If we stair at it for a bit, the following should be intuitive:

  • First term: change in components, what we shall expect in Euclidean space. It measures how the components of \(Y\) change. If the manifold were flat and the basis vectors didn’t vary, this would be enough.
  • Second term: correction for changes in basis, because on a curved manifold, the basis vectors \( rac{\partial}{\partial x^j}\) change from point to point. So even if the components \(Y^j\) stay constant, the vector field itself might change because the basis is rotating or stretching. This change is captured by the second term including the Christoffel symbols, which measure how the coordinate frame twists and turns.

Consequently, covariant derivative is basically the combination of component change and basis change, and it’s obvious from above the Christoffel symbols entirely dictate the properties of the chosen connection.

4] ————— A Short Reflection and Discussion —————

Let’s do a short summary and discussion before we continue. Based on the above definition, what is an affine connection conceptually? Well, an affine connection gives us a way to compare vectors at nearby points, which later will turn out to be our abilities to define

  • 1] a notion of parallel transport
  • 2] a notion of geodesics
  • 3] a notion of curvature

It is the structure that tells us how to move vectors around on a manifold. Without it, differentiation makes no sense.

At the same time, notice that apriori we choose this arbitrary connection. There are infinitely many affine connections on a manifold. Why? Recall the local definition of connection in coordinates \( abla_{X_i} X_j = \Gamma^k_{ij} X_k\). The Christoffel symbols \(\Gamma^k_{ij}\) can be any smooth functions. This already tells us that there are infinitely many possible connections. Nothing forces a specific choice at this stage. In fact, If \( abla\) is a connection and \(A\) is any smooth \((1,2)\)-tensor field, then \( ilde{ abla}_XY = abla_XY + A(X, Y)\) is another connection. So the space of connections is affine, not linear and thus there is enormous freedom. This is why it’s called “affine connection”.

It’s only after we impose some natural geometric conditions that one becomes canonical (more to reveal below). And after introducing Riemmanian metric, there’re specific properties of connection that we demand which render only one unique option left (kind of miracle). That’s why we do not drown in choices.

But let me also reinforce the idea that at this stage, no Riemannian metric at all is assumed. The definition of connection has nothing to do with any metric (we will see shortly that in some sense we do need to marry these two notions in a nice way; if this is your intuition, keep immersing yourself in it).

Another crucial point is that, you might object that after all why can’t we just subtract tangent vectors using the ambient Euclidean structure? If you visualize a manifold as a curved surface floating in 3D, there’s clearly a nice inner product structure already defined in \(\mathbb{R}^3\) which we effortlessly borrow.

Well, this is the deeper part, echoeing all the way back to why we even want a manifold. Recall that a general manifold has no built-in shape. A smooth manifold is just a set of points with coordinate charts which are glued together smoothly. That’s it. There’s no concept of length, angle, curvature, or embedding. Just smooth structure. We should NOT think of a general manifold as a curved surface floating in space. That is only a visualization aid.

Shape appears only after adding extra structure. For a smooth manifold, only differentiability exists. We can talk about vector fields, Lie brackets, and differential forms. But there’s no metric yet. Now if we add an affine connection, we could then ifferentiate vector fields (and also define parallel transport and talk about geodesics, see below). However, there’s still no notion of length or angle. It’s only after we introduce a Riemannian metric that we get a notion of lengths, angles, volumes, and curvature. Only here does something resembling “shape” emerge. And remarkably, all of this can be defined without embedding.

But before Riemannian metric, for a generic smooth manifold, embeddings are misleading. When we picture a sphere in \(\mathbb{R}^3\) or torus in space, we are seeing extrinsic geometry. But intrinsic geometry ignores how the object sits in space. A famous example is imagine a flat sheet of paper vs a cylinder made by rolling it. They look totaly different in \(\mathbb{R}^3\), but intrinsically they are the same geometry, because distances measured along the surface are unchanged. Consequently, intrinsic geometry does not care about bending in ambient space.

Recall that Gauss proved in his Theorema Egregium that

Curvature of a surface can be computed entirely from the metric.

(Here Curvature specifically refers to Gaussian Curvature). It does not depend on how the surface sits in space. This was revolutionary, only to be pushed further by Riemann later that:

Manifolds need not be embedded at all.

They can exist abstractly.

TODO: That means to talk about intrinsic geometry we need to define a metric first? Also, the definition of Riemannian metric has nothing to do with the embedding.

Consequently, if you are still thinking that a manifold is a curved surface in space, it might be helpful if you instead interpret manifold as an abstract smooth space and geometry is an additional structure placed on it. And more importantly,

The metric determines what “curved” even means.

The manifold is just an abstract collection of points with smooth structure, not an object with a fixed geometric shape. Shape emerges only after adding a metric.

Now, with the above discussions, suppose we do have an embedding: \(M \in \mathbb{R}^n\). We embed it. Vectors lie in tangent planes inside \(\mathbb{R}^n\). Then yes, we can subtract them as vectors in \(\mathbb{R}^n\). So what’s the problem? Well, it lies in again the insight Gauss offered back in 1827: intrinsic vs extrinsic geometry.

Let \(V(t) \in T_{\alpha(t)}M\). If we compute the ordinary derivative \( rac{dV}{dt}\) (pretend that \(M\) never has existed), it sure lives in in \(\mathbb{R}^n\) , but generally \( rac{dV}{dt} otin T_{\alpha(t)}M\). It usually has a normal component. That normal component measures how the surface bends in space. But that bending depends on the specific embedding we chose.

For example, on the sphere, if we carry a tangent vector along a great circle, the ambient derivative will point slightly inward (the normal component). But that normal part has nothing to do with intrinsic geometry, and it only reflects how the sphere sits in space. To get intrinsic change, we would take ambient derivative and project back to the tangent plane. (Spoiler alert: this projection then defines the Levi-Civita connection of the induced metric)

However, projecting back to tangent space gives intrinsic derivative.

  • Different embeddings give different projections.
  • Intrinsic geometry should not depend on embedding.

Connections defined intrinsically avoid embedding dependence.

To answer the question why we cannot always use this trick?

Not all manifolds come with a preferred embedding. Manifolds might be embedded but the embedding is not unique. Different embeddings give different ambient derivatives and the induced extrinsic correction indeed depends on embedding.

On the other hand, intrinsic geometry should not depend on how we sit in space. If we rely on ambient subtraction, we’re measuring extrinsic curvature. But Riemannian geometry is centered around exploring intrinsic properties. The connection should not depend on embedding.

Using Euclidean subtraction measures the intrinsic change plus the extrinsic bending, whereas the covariant derivative isolates and includes intrinsic change only.

TODO: This leads to yet another interesting observation: is covariant derivative just a projection of the Euclidean derivative back onto the manifold?

Enough deliberation for now, let’s continue our story.

5] Covariant Derivative Along a Curve

In many scenarios, we are interested in how a vector field “changes” on a specific trajectory. With an affine connection defined as above, how could we calculate such change?

Given a curve \(\alpha(t)\) and vector field along the curve: \(V(t) \in T_{\alpha(t)}M\). Again, the ordinary derivative $ rac{dV}{dt}$ makes no sense because:

\[ rac{dV}{dt} = \lim_{h ightarrow 0} rac{V(t + h) - V(t)}{h}\]

but \(V(t+v) \in T_{\alpha(t+h)}M\), \(V(t) \in T_{\alpha(t)}M\), and subtraction requires vectors to live in the same space.

To resolve this conflict and apply the defined connection, we define:

\[ rac{DV}{dt} = abla_{\alpha'(t)} V\]

where \(\alpha'(t) = rac{d\alpha}{dt} \in T_{\alpha(t)}M\).

which is called the covariant derivative. In other words, the covariant derivative along a curve is just the affine connection applied in the direction of the velocity vector. It’s not new machinery — it’s just the connection specialized to a curve.

In coordinates, if we write \(V(t) = V^k(t) rac{\partial}{\partial x^k}\):

\[ rac{DV^k}{dt} = ( rac{dV^k}{dt} + \Gamma^k_{ij} rac{dx^i}{dt} V^j) rac{\partial}{\partial x^k}\]

This is just the previous formula about coordinate representation of connections if we replace \(X = \alpha'(t)\). Consequently, we could carry the same interpretation: Covariant derivative = change in components+change in basis.

We could interpret it this way: Imagine walking on a sphere. We carry an arrow that we try to keep “pointing in the same direction.” Even if the arrow looks constant to you, from an external viewpoint it may be rotating because:

The tangent plane itself rotates as you move.

The coordinate basis changes.

So \( rac{D V}{dt}\) actually measures the true rate of change of the vector relative to/correcting for the manifold’s geometry. There are two kinds of change: 1)Intrinsic change — the vector itself changes’ 2) Fake change — the coordinate frame changes. But \( rac{D V}{dt}\) removes the fake change. It measures intrinsic change.

6] Parallel Vector Field and Parallel Transport

Then the natural question to ask is what does it mean to have \( rac{DV}{dt} = 0\)?

Well, there’s a specific name for it — the vector field \(V\) such \( rac{DV}{dt} = 0\) for a specific curve \(c\) is called a parallel vector field along the \(c\). Geometrically, this means that the vector is being transported without turning according to the manifold’s geometry.

On the other hand, the vector \(V(t)\) on \(V\) is said to parallel transport along \(c\).

Parallel transport is obtained by solving the following system of ODE:

\[ rac{dV^k}{dt} + \Gamma^k_{ij} rac{dx^i}{dt}V^j = 0\]

Notice that this is just \(n\) first order equations. Pointwise Christoffel data determines global comparison via integration.

This offers us another way to think of $ rac{D V}{dt}$:

Take \(V(t+h)\), parallel transport it back to \(T_{\alpha(t)}M\), subtract \(V(t)\), divide by $h$. Then take the limit.

This is the hidden geometric definition.

7] Geodesics

Closely building upon the above idea of parallelism, we define a curve to be a geodesic if:

\[ rac{D\alpha'}{dt} = 0\]

Meaning:

The velocity vector transports itself parallelly.

Geometric, this means that there is no intrinsic acceleration and the curve does not “turn” inside the manifold. This generalizes straight lines.

Another physical way to interpret this is that a geodesic has only acceleration perpendicular to the surface.

A quick sanity check: In Euclidean space \(\mathbb{R}\), since the basis vectors then Christoffel symbols vanish.

Consequently, \( rac{DV}{dt} = rac{dV}{dt}\) — Covariant derivative reduces to ordinary derivative, and this demonstrates that the definition of connection is truly a generalization.

And if we solve

\[ rac{d^2\alpha^k}{dt^2} + \Gamma^k_{ij} rac{dx^i}{dt}\alpha^j = 0\]

where we plug in \(V^j = \alpha^j\),

we will arrive at a straight line, which is the straight line in Euclidean space.

8] Metric Compatibility

There’re several ways to characterize metric compatibility. Let’s start with the following: a connection \( abla\) is compatible with a given metric \(<,>\) if

\[X \langle Y,Z angle = \langle abla_X Y, Z angle + \langle Y, abla_X Z angle\]

This must hold for all vector fields \(X,Y,Z,\in \mathfrak{X}(M)\). Intuitively, this represents that the derivative of the inner product equals the inner product of the derivatives. In other words, the connection differentiates vectors in a way that respects the metric. Note that here’s where we start to marry connection with metric!

Another way to look at metric compatibility is through the lens of geodesic, which I think more closely epitomizes the essence of this concept: If a vector field \(V(t)\) along a curve satisfies \( rac{dV}{dt} = 0\) (i.e., it’s parallelly transported), then metric compatibility implies:

\[ rac{d}{dt}<V(t), V(t)> = 0\]

which means that the length of the geodesic \(V(t)\) stays constant.

More generally, if both \(V\) and \(W\) are parallel, then

\[ rac{d}{dt}<V(t), W(t)> = 0\]

then the angles are also preserved.

Consequently, this is equivalent to saying that, in coordinates,

\[ abla_k g_{ij} = 0\]

or just

\[ abla g = 0\]

This means that the metric tensor has zero covariant derivative. Consequently, the metric looks “constant” under parallel transport. Notice that this generalizes the idea that in Euclidean space \(\partial_k​ \delta_{ij} = 0\).

A physical analogy will be that imagine each tangent space is a tiny rigid measuring device. Metric compatibility means that when we transport a measuring stick along a curve, its length does not change. If it did change, our notion of length would depend on the path we used to move it. That would be geometrically inconsistent.

Intuitively, metric compatibility suggests that when we move vectors around using the connection, we are not distorting the metric structure. There’s no stretching, shrinking, nor skewing of angles. Metric compatibility ensures that parallel transport acts like an orthogonal transformation between tangent spaces. If we move a basis along a curve via parallel transport, it stays orthonormal. Or in other words, parallel transport behaves like rigid motion inside each tangent space. This is perhaps the cleanest geometric picture.

And this comes not for free. We demand it by imposing such constraint. In general, connections are arbitrary. Most connections will change the length of a vector during parallel transport or distort angles. That would mean that the connection and the metric are fighting each other. However, metric compatibility forces harmony.

9] Torsion-Free (Symmetry)

We now understand that a connection is a rule for differentiating, and metric compatibility is to ensure that we preserve lengths and angles. There’s only last remaning piece before we narrow down our infinite choices of connection.

The formal definition of torsion-free, or symmetric, starts with the torsion tensor:

\[T(X,Y) = abla_X Y - abla_Y X - [X,Y]\]

If \(T = 0\), then

\[ abla_X Y - abla_Y X = [X,Y]\]

We call such connection torsion-free. In coordinate, we would have

\[\Gamma^k_{ij} = \Gamma^k_{ji}\]

that’s why many people also call it symmetric. Notice that the above equation is straightforward since coordinate vector fields (coming from a coordinate chart) always commute (\([ rac{\partial}{\partial x^i}, rac{\partial}{\partial x^j}] = 0\); Lie bracket has nothing to do with a connection apriori). Then just plug in \( abla_{X_i} X_j - abla_{X_j} X_i = 0\) and the above resulst should surface naturally. In short, the symmetry of Christoffel symbols comes from both torsion-free condition and the fact that coordinate vector fields commute.

Let me state it clearly: Coordinate vector fields always commute, independent of connection. Torsion-free means the connection respects that commutation structure. The connection does not determine the Lie bracket since the Lie bracket exists before any connection is chosen. Likewise, torsion-free does not make the Lie bracket zero — it only ensures that the connection respects whatever the Lie bracket already is.

This formula appears very bizarre, what does it mean? Recall that the Lie bracket between two vector fields \([X, Y]\) measures failure of flows to commute. If we first flow along \(X\) and then \(Y\), v.s. first \(Y\) and then \(X\), the difference is the Lie bracket: it measures intrinsic non-commutativity of directions. Now we look at \( abla_X Y - abla_Y X\). This is what the connection says about the difference between directions.

Consequently, Torsion compares between

  • What the connection thinks the commutator is, and
  • What the actual geometric commutator (Lie bracket) is

and that’s why it measures how much the connection artificially twists the geometry beyond the natural commutator.

An intuitive picture is that imagine walking on a surface. Take two small steps, first in direction \(X\), and then in direction \(Y\), and compare with reversing the order. There are two possible reasons the final positions differ:

  • The surface itself curves (intrinsic geometry), or
  • The connection is twisting things an extra amount.

Torsion measures the second. If torsion = 0, the connection introduces no artificial twisting. All the remaning non-commutativity comes purely from the underlying geometry itself, which is exactly what we want.

On the other way around, if we choose a connection with torsion. Then even though \([X_i, X_j] = 0\), we would have \( abla_{X_i} X_j - abla_{X_j} X_i eq 0\). That “extra part” would be torsion. Consequently, we could say that torsion measures deviation from symmetry beyond the intrinsic commutation.

Why does Riemannian geometry prefer torsion-free? Since Riemannian geometry models distance and angles, there is no natural “twisting” built into length geometry: the most natural connection is metric compatible and torsion-free, which ensures that geodesics behave like natural straightest paths.

In summary, a connection that is torsion-free does not introduce any artificial twisting beyond the natural commutation of vector fields.

10] Levi-Civita Connection

From the above, we know that metric compatibility controls stretching, whereas torsion-free controls twisting. Together they mean that the connection is the most natural differentiation compatible with smooth geometry and measurement.

Indeed, now we will manually impose

  1. Metric compatibility
  2. Torsion-free

And, remarkably, there only exists exactly one such connection, which is called the Levi-Civita connection.

As demanded by its defining property it removes stretching and twisting. Only intrinsic curvature remains.

It could be shown without too much effort that the Levi-Civita connection entirely depends on the selected metric. The Christoffel symbols of the Levi-Civita Connection are thus entirely determined by the components of the metric $g_{ij}$ and their first derivatives:

\[ oxed{\Gamma^k_{ij} = rac{1}{2} g^{k\ell} \left( rac{\partial g_{j\ell}}{\partial x^i} + rac{\partial g_{i\ell}}{\partial x^j} - rac{\partial g_{ij}}{\partial x^\ell} ight)}\]

(or if we just use the other notation: \(\Gamma^k_{ij} = rac{1}{2}g^{k\ell} \left( X_i(g_{j\ell}) + X_j(g_{i\ell}) - X_\ell(g_{ij}) ight)\))

How do we obtain the above equation? We just directly calculate the Christoffel symbols using the metric compatibility and torsion-free conditions. It turns out to be not as messy as you might expect:

By definition of the connection coefficients:

\[ abla_{X_i} X_j = \Gamma^k_{ij} X_k.\]

By torsion-free condition we will have

\[T(X_i,X_j) = abla_{X_i} X_j - abla_{X_j} X_i - [X_i,X_j] = 0.\]

Since this is a coordinate frame, \([X_i,X_j] = 0\). Torsion-free implies: \( abla_{X_i} X_j = abla_{X_j} X_i\), so \(\Gamma^k_{ij} = \Gamma^k_{ji}\). Thus the lower indices are symmetric (remember I stated this property above!)

By metric compatibility, we would have \( abla g = 0\). This means that

\[X_i(g_{jk}) = g( abla_{X_i}X_j, X_k) + g(X_j, abla_{X_i}X_k).\]

Now if we substitute the definition of \(\Gamma_{ij}^l\), since \( abla_{X_i} X_j = \Gamma^l_{ij} X_l\), we get:

\[g( abla_{X_i}X_j, X_k) = \Gamma^\ell_{ij} g_{\ell k}\]

and

\[g(X_j, abla_{X_i}X_k) = \Gamma^\ell_{ik} g_{j\ell}\]

Thus:

\[X_i(g_{jk}) = \Gamma^\ell_{ij} g_{\ell k} + \Gamma^\ell_{ik} g_{j\ell}\]

Now comes a little tricky step: we write out the above formula but permute the indices (since they are choosen arbitrarily). We obtain:

(1) \(X_i(g_{jk}) = \Gamma^\ell_{ij} g_{\ell k} + \Gamma^\ell_{ik} g_{j\ell}\)

(2) \(X_j(g_{ik}) = \Gamma^\ell_{ji} g_{\ell k} + \Gamma^\ell_{jk} g_{i\ell}\)

(3) \(X_k(g_{ij}) = \Gamma^\ell_{ki} g_{\ell j} + \Gamma^\ell_{kj} g_{i\ell}\)

Then, using the symmetry we obtained above from torsion-free condition, since

\[\Gamma^\ell_{ij} = \Gamma^\ell_{ji},\]

compute

\[(1) + (2) - (3).\]

The feft-hand side becomes

\[X_i(g_{jk}) + X_j(g_{ik}) - X_k(g_{ij})\]

Right-hand side simplifies to:

\[2\,\Gamma^\ell_{ij} g_{\ell k}.\]

Consequently,

\[X_i(g_{jk}) + X_j(g_{ik}) - X_k(g_{ij}) = 2\,\Gamma^\ell_{ij} g_{\ell k}.\]

By rearranging terms and multiplying by the inverse metric \(g^{km}\):

\[g^{km} g_{\ell k} = \delta^m_\ell.\]

We obtain:

\[\Gamma^m_{ij} = rac{1}{2} g^{mk} \left(X_i(g_{jk})+X_j(g_{ik})-X_k(g_{ij}) ight).\]

Renaming dummy indices:

\[ oxed{ \Gamma^k_{ij}= rac{1}{2}g^{k\ell}\left(X_i(g_{j\ell})+X_j(g_{i\ell})-X_\ell(g_{ij}) ight)}\]

Remark: This formula relies crucially on the fact that the frame ${X_i}$ is a coordinate frame, meaning:

\[[X_i, X_j] = 0.\]

If instead we used a general (non-coordinate) frame where

\[[X_i, X_j] eq 0,\]

then additional terms involving the structure constants of the frame would appear in the formula for the connection. Thus the classical Christoffel formula holds precisely because the coordinate vector fields commute.

11] Quick Summary

  • Connection: Infinitesimal rule for comparing tangent spaces.
  • Christoffel symbols: Local coefficients describing how frames twist.
  • Metric compatibility: No stretching under transport.
  • Torsion-free: No artificial twisting.
  • Levi-Civita connection: Unique natural connection for a Riemannian metric.
  • Intrinsic geometry: Independent of embedding.

12] ————— Discussions —————

When I first learned about connection/parallel transport/geodesics, I have tons of questions and different topics seem to mingle with one another, each defying the others’ validity. After months of delibration and study, I finally figured out the inner workings of these concepts, and I have to admit that I’m still deepening my understanding.

I’ll not regurgitate statements and clarifications made in the previous “short summary” section 4], but instead think back on a few other critical questions to ponder. I highly recommen that readers go through 4] before reading this section.

1) Why do connections exist even without metrics?

I had this thought which I deemed natural and hard to wrap my head aroud: If connections are about measuring changes, and metrics measure geometry, why can a connection exist without a metric?

Well, let’s stay back a ste and think towards what a connection does and does NOT do. Notice that a connection gives us a way to differentiate vector fields, to compare nearby tangent spaces. From there the notion of parallel transport and geodesics. However, notice that there’s nowhere we mentioned lengths or angles. A connection is a rule about how vectors move, not about how long they are. It only needs to satisfies linearity in \(X\) and Leibniz rule in \(Y\). That’s purely algebraic and smooth structure, and there is no inner product in the definition. So logically:

Connections exist on any smooth manifold; but metrics are optional.

Furthermore, differentiation itself does not require a metric. Think about perhaps ordinary calculus. When we compute \( rac{df}{dx}\), we do not need an inner product. Differentiation only needs smooth structure.

Similarly, on a manifold, to differentiate vector fields, we only need a mooth structure, a rule for comparing tangent spaces which we call/define as the connection. A metric is not logically required for this.

A simple analogy is roads vs ruler: Imagine that the manifold is a landscape. A connection tells us how to “carry arrows” while walking, and a metric tells us how long arrows are. We can describe how an arrow rotates along a path without knowing its length.

A concrete example here: Lie group. Take a Lie group \(G\), we can define a connection using left translation:

\[ abla_XY = 0\]

for left-invariant vector fields. This defines parallel transport algebraically, but there’s no metric involved. This works because the group structure already lets use compare tangent spaces.

In Riemannian geometry, we care about length-minimizing curves and curvature measured from metric, and that’s why we impose metric compatibility and torsion-free which produces the unique Levi-Civita connection. Howver, that’s a specialization.

In some sense, connections are more primitive than metrics, because

we can define curvature and geodesics from a connection alone.

Gauge theory in physics uses connections without metrics since metrics are additional structure, whereas connections are about differentiation — a more basic concept. A mental picture I have is that

  • Smooth structure \( ightarrow\) allows calculus
  • Connection \( ightarrow\) allows differentiation of vector fields
  • Metric \( ightarrow\) allows measurement

Each layer adds new capability and they are independent choices.

2) Point-wise definition of Christoffel symbols?

Another concern that haunted me for quite a while is the following: Now hopefully we are clear that connection is entirely dependent upon the Christoffel symbols, and connection is meant to differentiate vector fields at different points. Yet based on the definition of Christoffel symbols, it seems that it’s just using the connection upon pairwise basis vectors at the same exact point. So how can something defined pointwise encode comparison between different tangent spaces?

Well, there’re several persectives to look at this.

Firstly, the hidden “different points” are actually there already: when we compute \( abla_XY\), we are not subtracting \(Y(p)\) and \(Y(q)\) directly. Instead, we are asking: If I move an infinitesimal step from \(p\) in direction \(X\), how does \(Y\) change? This “infinitesimal move” already involves neighboring points. So even though the formula is evaluated at \(p\), it contains directional information about nearby points. Moreover, Christoffel symbols tell us how the frame twists and turns as we move. Once we know how the frame moves, we can differentiate any vector field. The “comparison across points” is encoded in how the frame changes.

Secondly, notice that a connection does not directly subtract vectors at different points. Instead, it gives an “infinitesimal rule” for how vector fields change. Indeed, a connection describes infinitesimal change. The comparison between different points comes from integrating this infinitesimal rule.

A simple analogy in \(\mathbb{R}^n\). The derivative of a function is defined by \(f'(x)\), which is computed at a single point. Yet from this local rule, we can compare values at different points by integrating:

\[f(b) - f(a) = \int_a^b f'(t)\,dt\]

so local derivative \( ightarrow\) global comparison. The same story here: Christoffel symbols encode infinitesimal twisting of the frame, as the definition shows:

\[ abla_{X_i} X_j = \Gamma^k_{ij} X_k\]

This is indeed pointwise. But what does it mean? It tells us how the coordinate basis vector \( rac{\partial}{\partial x^j}\) changes when we move infinitesimally in direction \( rac{\partial}{\partial x^i}\). Consequently, even though the expression is evaluated at a single point, it describes variation in a direction. That direction information encodes how things change from one point to nearby points. Then that means we are integrating these infinitesimal changes?

Exactly! Parallel transport along a curve is obtained by solving

\[ rac{DV}{dt} = 0\]

To be more concrete, if we think in coordinates, we are solving

\[ oxed{ rac{dV^k}{dt} + \Gamma^k_{ij} rac{dx^i}{dt} V^j = 0 }\]

for all \(k\). This is an ODE! Given initial vector \(V(0)\), the Christoffel symbols determine \(V(t)\) uniquely. Consequently, they determine how vectors are transported from one point to another.

In summary we could think of Christoffel symbols as the “connection coefficients” describing how the local coordinate grid bends and twists. They encode infinitesimal change, from which we build parallel transport, geodesics, and later curvature. Consequently, yes indeed that Christoffel symbols are defined pointwise because connections describe infinitesimal variation, and infinitesimal variation is all we need to compare vectors at different points via integration.

TODO Now our earlier question becomes clearer:

If we define derivatives using an embedding, we are using extra structure.

But intrinsic geometry should depend only on data defined on the manifold itself.

That’s why the Levi-Civita connection is defined purely from the metric.

No embedding required.

Theoretical (sanity check) questions to ponder: 1] Why curvature is intrinsic? Does intrinsic meaning only in need of a metric? Is metric required for intrinsic geometry or curvature? 2] Why do we say that we can define curvature and geodesics from a connection alone 3] Or how geodesics make sense without embedding 4] How curvature arises from metric compatibility

entry.title: The Flow of Time: Prob/Measure/Transport/Generative Mumble(5): Optimal Transport Flow: the Kantorovich Problem

link.title: The Flow of Time: Prob/Measure/Transport/Generative Mumble(5): Optimal Transport Flow: the Kantorovich Problem

entry.content:
1] Kantorovich Problem

1.1] Defintion

Notice that if a transport map exists, then the Monge Problem solution is larger than or equal to that of the Kantorovich.

1.2] Contrast with Monge Problem

1.3] Existence of Solution

1.4] Convexity

2] The Kantorovich Dual

2.1] Problem Formulation

2.2] The C-Transform

2.2.1] Definition

2.2.2] Relation with Legendre Transform

It’s useful to understand the Legendre transform from several viewpoints which appear in convex analysis, symplectic/differential geometry, and optimal transport duality. They are mathematically equivalent but emphasize different structures.

2.2.2.1] Convex analysis viewpoint (Fenchel–Legendre transform)

Let \(f : \mathbb{R}^n o (-\infty,\infty]\).

The Legendre transform (or convex conjugate) is

\[f^*(p) = \sup_{x \in \mathbb{R}^n} \left( p \cdot x - f(x) ight)\]

This means that for each vector \(p\), we measure how well the linear function

\[x \mapsto p \cdot x\]

can dominate \(f(x)\).

Equivalently,

\[f(x) \ge p \cdot x - f^*(p)\]

for all \(x\).

So \(f^*(p)\) describes the supporting hyperplane with slope \(p\). In other words, \(f^*(p)\) is the intercept of the function \(p \cdot x\).

Indeed, the affine function

\[\ell_p(x) = p \cdot x - f^*(p)\]

satisfies

\[\ell_p(x) \le f(x)\]

for all \(x\).

On the other hand, \(f\) can be thus reconstructed as

\[f(x) = \sup_p ig( p\cdot x - f^*(p) ig)\]

This is the biconjugate theorem:

\[f^{**} = f\]

for convex lower semicontinuous \(f\).

If \(f\) is smooth and strictly convex,

\[p = abla f(x)\]

and

\[x = abla f^*(p).\]

Thus

\[ abla f : x \mapsto p\]

is inverted by

\[ abla f^*.\]

This is extremely important in information geometry and mirror descent.

2.2.2.2] Differential Geometry Viewpoint

Another way to interpret Legendre transform is to consider it as a change of coordinates in the cotangent bundle.

Consider the graph

\[(x, abla f(x))\]

inside

\[\mathbb{R}^n_x imes \mathbb{R}^n_p.\]

This is a submanifold of the cotangent bundle

\[T^*\mathbb{R}^n.\]

Define the function

\[F(x,p) = p\cdot x - f(x).\]

The Legendre transform arises when we maximize over (x).

Consequently, the geometric picture is the following:

The graph of \(df\)

\[p = abla f(x)\]

is a Lagrangian submanifold in the symplectic manifold

\[(T^*\mathbb{R}^n, \omega = dp \wedge dx).\]

The Legendre transform simply reparametrizes this same Lagrangian manifold using the variable \(p\) instead of \(x\).

So:

  • \(f(x)\) describes the manifold using x-coordinates
  • \(f^*(p)\) describes the same manifold using p-coordinates

This viewpoint is fundamental in:

  • classical mechanics
  • symplectic geometry
  • Hamiltonian/Lagrangian duality.
2.2.2.3] Classical Mechanics Example

In Lagrangian mechanics

\[L(q,v)\]

define momentum

\[p = rac{\partial L}{\partial v}.\]

Then the Hamiltonian is

\[H(q,p) = \sup_v \left( p\cdot v - L(q,v) ight).\]

This is exactly a Legendre transform in the velocity variable.

2.2.2.4] Optimal Transport Viewpoint

Now we arrive at why this matters for optimal transport. In Kantorovich duality, we define the c-transform: Given a cost function \(c(x,y)\), we define

\[\phi^c(y) = \inf_x \left(c(x,y) - \phi(x) ight).\]

Comparing this with Legendre transform:

\[f^*(p) = \sup_x (p\cdot x - f(x)).\]

This is a c-transform with cost

\[c(x,p) = -x\cdot p.\]

Thus

Legendre transform = special case of c-transform with linear cost.. Specifically, for quadratic cost

\[c(x,y) = rac{|x-y|^2}{2}\]

the c-transform becomes

\[\phi^c(y) = \inf_x \left( rac{|x-y|^2}{2} - \phi(x) ight).\]

Then optimal transport potentials satisfy

\[\phi^{cc} = \phi.\]

This mirrors the property

\[f^{**} = f\]

in convex analysis.

Consequently, there’s also a deep connection between the gradient map \( abla f\) and the optimal transport map (see below!)

You might wonder then why would Legendre transform appear in OT? Well, if we dissect the quadratic cost

\[c(x,y)= rac{|x-y|^2}{2}\]

as

\[ rac{|x-y|^2}{2} = rac{|x|^2}{2} + rac{|y|^2}{2} - x\cdot y.\]

It’s not hard to see that the OT dual problem essentially contains a hidden Legendre transform.

That is why Brenier’s theorem eventually gives the map

\[T(x) = abla \psi(x)\]

for a convex potential \(\psi\).

2.2.2.5] Intuition That Unifies Everything

The Legendre transform switches between two equivalent descriptions of the same object: while the variable \((x, f(x))\) represent the function values, \((p, f^*(p))\) indicate the supporting hyperplanes.

In optimal transport, the same idea becomes that \((x, \phi(x))\) represent the potentials, whereas \((y, \phi^c(y))\) indicate the cost-adapted hyerplanes — the c-transform is simply the Legendre transform adapted to the geometry induced by the transport cost.

This single most important example $$ c(x,y)= rac{ x-y^2}{2} $$ reveals why optimal maps are gradients of convex functions.

2.3]
entry.summary: Static and Dynamic Formulation of Optimal Transport Flow, Duality, Kantorovich Problem

entry.title: The Flow of Time: Prob/Measure/Transport/Generative Mumble(X+1): Entropy Convexity and Ricci Curvature

link.title: The Flow of Time: Prob/Measure/Transport/Generative Mumble(X+1): Entropy Convexity and Ricci Curvature

entry.content:
This blog post is dedicated to a specific topic that unites entropy, displacement convexity, and Ricci curvature. In my personal opinion, this is one of the deepest and aesthetically appealing results of modern optimal transport theory. I quickly alluded to it in the 4th blog post in this series, but here I will expand from there a little more.

In summary, what I will elaborate is where optimal transport becomes shockingly close to Riemannian geometry. The key idea is due to Felix Otto: the space of probability densities with the 2-Wasserstein metric behaves like an infinite-dimensional Riemannian manifold, and the entropy Hessian in this manifold contains the Ricci tensor of the base manifold.

In case we get confused, in this blog I will specifically add subscripts to distinguish differentiation on \(t\) vs on \(x\).

1] The “manifold” of probability densities

Let

\[(M,g)\]

be a smooth \(d\)-dimensional Riemannian manifold with Riemannian metric \(g\).

Let \(dx\) denote the Riemannian volume measure on \(M\).

We consider the space

\[\mathcal P_2(M) = \left\{ ho(x) \ge 0 : \int_M ho(x)\,dx = 1, \quad \int_M |x|^2 ho(x)\,dx < \infty ight\}.\]

Each element of \(\mathcal P_2(M)\) is a probability density function

\[ ho : M ightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}.\]

We treat (\mathcal P_2(M)) as an infinite-dimensional manifold.

2] Curves of probability densities

Consider a time-dependent probability density

\[ ho_t(x), \quad t \in [0,1],\; x \in M.\]

Thus we have a map (probability flow):

\[ ho : [0,1] imes M ightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}, \quad (t,x) \mapsto ho_t(x).\]

Assume mass is conserved over time:

\[\int_M ho_t(x)\,dx = 1 \quad orall t.\]

Mass conservation implies the continuity equation

\[\partial_t ho_t(x) + abla \cdot \left( ho_t(x) v_t(x) ight) = 0.\]

Here

  • \(v_t(x) \in T_xM\) is a velocity vector field
  • \[v : [0,1] imes M o TM\]

3] Tangent vectors in probability space

At a fixed density \( ho_t(x)\), the time derivative

\[\dot{ ho}_t(x) = \partial_t ho_t(x)\]

acts as a tangent vector in the space of probability measures.

From the continuity equation, we know that

\[\dot{ ho}_t(x) = - abla \cdot \left( ho_t(x) v_t(x) ight).\]

Optimal transport theory shows that along Wasserstein geodesics, the velocity field is always a gradient field (we covered this in this previous blog post YY):

\[v_t(x) = abla \phi_t(x)\]

for some scalar potential

\[\phi_t : M o \mathbb{R}.\]

Thus tangent vectors take the form

\[\dot{ ho}_t(x) = - abla \cdot \left( ho_t(x) abla \phi_t(x) ight).\]

Therefore

  • tangent vectors acquires a corresponding scalar potentials \(\phi_t(x)\)

4] Otto’s Riemannian metric

Felix Otto introduced a Riemannian metric on \(\mathcal P_2(M)\).

Suppose we have two tangent vectors:

\[\dot{ ho}^{(1)}(x) = - abla \cdot \left( ho(x) abla \phi_1(x) ight)\]

and

\[\dot{ ho}^{(2)}(x) = - abla \cdot \left( ho(x) abla \phi_2(x) ight).\]

The Riemannian inner product is defined as

\[\langle \dot{ ho}^{(1)}, \dot{ ho}^{(2)} angle_{ ho} = \int_M g_x ig( abla \phi_1(x), abla \phi_2(x) ig) ho(x)\,dx.\]

Here

  • \(g_x(\cdot,\cdot)\) is the Riemannian metric at \(x\).

How to interpret this? Notice that the squared norm of a tangent vector becomes

\[\| \dot{ ho} \|_ ho^2 = \int_M | abla \phi(x) |_g^2 ho(x)\,dx.\]

This equals the kinetic energy of moving mass.

Indeed, the Benamou–Brenier formula states

\[W_2^2( ho_0, ho_1) = \inf_{ ho_t,v_t} \int_0^1 \int_M |v_t(x)|^2 ho_t(x) \,dx\,dt.\]

5] Wasserstein geodesic equations

Under Otto’s metric, geodesics satisfy the continuity equation:

\[\partial_t ho_t(x) + abla \cdot \left( ho_t(x) abla \phi_t(x) ight) = 0\]

By the Hamilton–Jacobi equation

\[\partial_t \phi_t(x) + rac12 | abla \phi_t(x) |^2 = 0.\]

Solving these equations produces the optimal transport interpolation (optimal transport flow) between

\[ ho_0(x) \quad ext{and} \quad ho_1(x).\]

Just as a sanity check, we should now be comfortable for connecting the static and dynamic view of optimal transport, and easily write out the optimal flow \( ho_t(x)\) as the law of the straight flow once we know the optimal map:

\[ ho_t(x) = ext{Law}((1-t)x + t\Phi^{opt}(x))\]

or, in other words

\[ ho_t = ((1-t) + t\Phi^{opt})_{\#}\]

6] Entropy Functional and Variations

Define the entropy functional as (note whether to take negative sign is just a choice; you could also call it negative entropy if you are familiar with the negative sign in front of the integral)

\[\mathrm{H}( ho) = \int_M ho(x)\log ho(x) \,dx.\]

This is a functional

\[\mathrm{H} : \mathcal P_2(M) ightarrow \mathbb{R}.\]

Then let’s compute the first variation:

\[ rac{\delta \mathrm{H}}{\delta ho}(x) = 1+\log ho(x).\]

The Wasserstein gradient flow equation for entropy then becomes

\[\partial_t ho_t(x) = abla \cdot \left( ho_t(x) abla rac{\delta \mathrm{H}}{\delta ho}( ho_t(x)) ight).\]

Substitute

\[ rac{\delta \mathrm{H}}{\delta ho}(x)= 1+\log ho_t(x).\]

Then

\[ abla (1+\log ho_t(x))= rac{ abla ho_t(x)}{ ho_t(x)}.\]

Thus

\[\partial_t ho_t(x) = abla \cdot \left( abla ho_t(x) ight).\]

Therefore

\[\partial_t ho_t(x)= \Delta_x ho_t(x).\]

Very surprisingly:

The heat equation is the gradient flow of entropy in Wasserstein space.

This result is known as the Jordan–Kinderlehrer–Otto (JKO) scheme.

7] Second Derivative of Entropy along a Geodesic

Let

\[\{ ho_t(x)\}_{t \, \in \, [0, 1]}\]

be a Wasserstein geodesic generated by the potential

\[\phi_t(x).\]

Compute

\[ rac{d^2}{dt^2} \mathrm{H}( ho_t).\]

After a long calculation using the continuity equation and the Bochner identity, we would obtain

\[ rac{d^2}{dt^2} \mathrm{H}( ho_t) = \int_M \Big( | abla^2 \phi_t(x)|^2 + \mathrm{Ric}_x ( abla \phi_t(x), abla \phi_t(x) ) \Big) ho_t(x) \,dx.\]

Here

  • ( abla^2\phi_t(x) ) is the Hessian of ( \phi_t )
  • ( \mathrm{Ric}_x(\cdot,\cdot) ) is the Ricci curvature tensor at (x).

How to interpret this formula then? Well, notice that the Hessian splits into two terms:

  • Positive Hessian term $$ abla^2\phi_t(x) ^2 \ge 0$$
  • Curvature term \(\mathrm{Ric}_x ( abla\phi_t(x), abla\phi_t(x))\), which depends on the geometry of the manifold.

8] Convexity from Ricci Curvature

Suppose the manifold satisfies

\[\mathrm{Ric}_x(v,v) \ge K|v|^2 \quad ext{for all } v\in T_xM.\]

Then

\[\mathrm{Ric}_x ( abla\phi_t(x), abla\phi_t(x) ) \ge K | abla\phi_t(x) |^2.\]

Thus

\[ rac{d^2}{dt^2} \mathrm{H}( ho_t) \ge K \int_M | abla\phi_t(x) |^2 ho_t(x) \,dx.\]

But along a Wasserstein geodesic,

\[\int_M | abla\phi_t(x) |^2 ho_t(x) \,dx = W_2^2( ho_0, ho_1)\]

Therefore

\[\mathrm{H}( ho_t) ext{ is } K ext{-convex along Wasserstein geodesics}.\]

9] Geometric Meaning

This shows that

\[ ext{Hessian}_{W_2} \mathrm{H} \sim \mathrm{Ric}.\]

So the Ricci curvature of the manifold controls the convexity of entropy in Wasserstein space. This insight leads to the Lott–Sturm–Villani theory, which defines curvature bounds using optimal transport.

Or put it differently, the Ricci curvature ≥ \(K\) if and only if

The entropy functional is (K)-convex along Wasserstein geodesics in the space of probability measures.

10] Lott–Sturm–Villani Theory

entry.title: The Flow of Time: Prob/Measure/Transport/Generative Mumble(X): Entropy and Fisher Information

link.title: The Flow of Time: Prob/Measure/Transport/Generative Mumble(X): Entropy and Fisher Information

entry.content:
1] Entropy

1.1] Entropy Definitions

In this sectio, I mean to clarify the relationships between entropy, negative entropy, and their convexity properties under different interpolations of probability distributions.

1.1.1] Discrete Shannon Entropy

For a discrete distribution:

\[H(p) = - \sum_i p_i \log p_i\]

This is the original Shannon entropy.

1.1.2] Differential Entropy (Continuous Case)

For a continuous density \(p(x)\):

\[H(p) = -\int p(x)\log p(x)\,dx\]

This is called differential entropy.

Important properties:

  • It depends on the coordinate system
  • It can be negative
  • It is not invariant under reparameterization

Note that this is not the continuous analogue of the discrete entropy (Shannon entropy).

1.1.3] Boltzmann / Gibbs Entropy

In statistical physics:

\[S = k_B \log W\]

where \(W\) is the number of microstates.

In distribution form (Gibbs entropy):

\[S = -k_B \int p(x)\log p(x)\,dx\]

Thus, up to the constant \(k_B\), this is the same functional as differential entropy.

1.1.4] Negative Entropy

Negative entropy is defined as

\[F(p) = \int p(x)\log p(x)\,dx\]

So

  • Entropy is concave
  • Negative entropy is convex

1.1.5] Relative Entropy

Relative entropy is also frequently known as and referred to by the name of Kullback Leibler (KL) Divergence:

\[D_{KL}(p|q) = \int p(x)\log\frac{p(x)}{q(x)}dx\]

This should be interpreted as the continuos counterpart of the discrete Shannon entropy. They are both scale invariant. I will elaborate this below.

1.2] Mixture Interpolation of Distributions

Given two distributions \(p\) and \(q\), their mixture interpolation is

\[\rho_t = (1-t)p + tq, \quad t \in [0,1].\]

Interpretation:

  • With probability \(1-t\), sample from \(p\)
  • With probability \(t\), sample from \(q\)

This is a straight line in the linear space of densities.

1.3] Convexity Under Mixtures

1.3.1] Negative Entropy is Mixture Convex

Remember the definition of the negative entropy:

\[F(\rho) = \int \rho(x)\log \rho(x)\,dx\]

For mixture interpolation

\[\rho_t = (1-t)\rho_0 + t\rho_1\]

we have

\[F(\rho_t) \le (1-t)F(\rho_0) + tF(\rho_1).\]

Therefore

Negative entropy is convex under mixtures.

The reason is that \(\phi(x)=x\log x\) is a convex function.

1.3.2] Entropy is Mixture Concave

Differential entropy is

\[H(\rho) = -\int \rho\log\rho.\]

Since it is the negative of a convex functional, then

\[H(\rho_t) \ge (1-t)H(\rho_0) + tH(\rho_1).\]

Thus Differential entropy is concave under mixtures.

1.4] Why Differential Entropy Depends on Coordinates

Now we have a general feel about different kinds of entropy and its convexity properties, you might have a natural question as to why the differential entropy is not a continuous counterpart of the discrete Shannon entropy. Afterall, it seems to be just taking limits. Also, what does it mean to say that differential entropy is not invariant under change of variables. Let’s investigate into this a little bit more.

Let

\[y = g(x).\]

The density transforms according to the Jacobian:

\[p_Y(y) = p_X(x)\left|\frac{dx}{dy}\right|.\]

Computing the entropy after transformation gives (this is not hard; consider as a little exercise for yourself)

\[H(Y) = H(X) + \mathbb{E}[\log |g'(X)|].\]

Thus differential entropy changes under reparameterization.

For example, let

\[X \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1).\]

Then, perhaps a little surprisingly,

\[H(X)=0.\]

Define \(Y=2X\), then \(Y\sim\text{Uniform}(0,2)\) and thus

\[H(Y)=\log 2\]

This demonstrates that simply changing the scale changes differential entropy.

Why does this happen? Well, differential (continuous) entropy involves densities:

\[p(x)=\frac{dP}{dx}.\]

Since the base measure \(dx\) depends on the coordinate system, entropy does too.

In other words, differential entropy is really entropy relative to the Lebesgue measure.

1.5] Coordinate-Invariant Quantities

The good news is, quantities involving density ratios remain invariant. For example: KL divergence

\[D_{KL}(p||q) = \int p(x)\log\frac{p(x)}{q(x)}dx.\]

Under coordinate change, Jacobians cancel:

\[\frac{p_Y(y)}{q_Y(y)} = \frac{p_X(x)}{q_X(x)}.\]

Thus, KL divergence is coordinate invariant.

1.6] Mixture Geometry vs Transport Geometry

Mixture interpolation does not move mass.

Example:

  • \(p\): Gaussian centered at \(-5\)
  • \(q\): Gaussian centered at \(5\)

Mixture interpolation produces two bumps, because the distributions coexist, but physical transport should produce one bump moving across space.

I also discussed this difference in the previous blog XX.

In optimal transport, particles move. If \(T(x)\) is the optimal transport map, then

\[x_t = (1-t)x + tT(x).\]

The density evolves via

\[\rho_t = ((1-t)\mathrm{Id}+tT)_\# \rho_0.\]

This path is the Wasserstein geodesic.

1.7] Displacement Convexity

The subjects of the following two sections have been discussed in the previous blog and I will expand them in the next post. For now, as a review and also to reinforce our interpreation, please allow me to state the findings again:

McCann (1997) proved that the functional (negative entropy)

\[U(\rho) = \int \rho\log\rho\]

is convex along Wasserstein geodesics:

\[U(\rho_t) \le (1-t)U(\rho_0) + tU(\rho_1).\]

Thus, negative entropy is displacement convex.

Since entropy is the negative of this functional,

\[H(\rho) = -U(\rho),\]

we obtain that entropy is displacement concave.

In summary, differnetial entropy is both mixture concave and displacement concave, and negative entropy is convex in both concepts.

1.8] Why Displacement Convexity Matters

Mixture interpolation corresponds to randomly choosing distributions, where optimal transport corresponds to moving mass in space.

This might sound trivial, but many physical and probabilistic dynamics follow transport geometry.

For example, the Fokker–Planck equation

\[\partial_t \rho = \nabla\cdot(\rho\nabla V)+\Delta\rho\]

Jordan–Kinderlehrer–Otto (1998) showed:

This PDE is the gradient flow of entropy in Wasserstein space.

This connects optimal transport, diffusion equations, statistical physics, and Riemannian geometry into a unified framework.

2] Fisher Information

entry.title: The Flow of Time: Prob/Measure/Transport/Generative Mumble(4): Optimal Transport Flow: the Monge Problem

link.title: The Flow of Time: Prob/Measure/Transport/Generative Mumble(4): Optimal Transport Flow: the Monge Problem

entry.content:
1] Optimal Transport Flow Problem Formulation

  • Dynamic Eulerian
  • Dynamic Lagragian
  • Static (Monge Problem)

2] Equivalence of Static and Dynamic Formulations

  • Dynamic <= Static

  • Static <= Dynamic

For Jensen’s inequality to reach equality, the integrad within the expectation has to be time-invariant, which means indicates that \(\Phi_t\) corresponds to a line.

Consequently,

\[\Phi_t(x) = (1-t)x + t\Phi^{opt}(x)\]

associates the optimal flow (dynamical view) with the optimal map (static view).

3] Convexity Issues

  • Claim: the dynamical problem is convex and constraint is linear (infinite-dimensional)

  • However, for the static problem, the objective function is convex, but the constraint is not linear.

4] Dynamic Problem Solution

4.1] Proof

5] Displacement Convexity and Ricci Curvature

This section summarizes what I think is one of the deepest discoveries and constructions in modern optimal transport theory.

\(t ightarrow H[ ho_t]\) is convex.

Implications for convexity

  • Ricci flow to define curvature

(I will have another blog to elaborate on this amazing result)

5.1] Ordinary Convexity in Probability Space

If we view probability measures as a linear space, the natural interpolation between two measures \(\mu\) and \( u\) is the mixture interpolation:

\[ ho_t = (1-t)\mu + t u, \quad t \in [0,1].\]

A functional \(F( ho_t)\) is convex in the usual sense if

\[F ig((1-t)\mu + t u ig) \le (1-t)F(\mu) + tF( u).\]

This is sometimes called mixture convexity. For example, negative entropy is convex under mixtures.

Specifically, recall that the negative entropy is

\[F( ho) = \int ho(x) \log ho(x)\, dx .\]

(so the usual notion of entropy is \(H( ho) = -F( ho)\)). Then we have

\[F((1-t)p + t q) \le (1-t)F(p) + tF(q).\]

Substituting \(F( ho)=\int ho\log ho\),

\[\int ho_t \log ho_t \le (1-t)\int p\log p + t\int q\log q .\]

This is very intuitive, as entropy measures uncertainty, and mixing two distributions increases uncertainty, so entropy goes up:

\[H((1-t)p + tq) \ge (1-t)H(p) + tH(q).\]

Or, we could interpret this algebraically, since

\[\phi(x) = x \log x\]

is convex for \(x>0\).

Since

\[F( ho) = \int \phi( ho(x)) dx,\]

and integrals preserve convexity, $F$ becomes a convex functional.

Thus:

  • Entropy is (mixture) concave
  • Negative entropy is (mixture) convex

This property is fundamental because:

  • It makes KL divergence a Bregman divergence of negative entropy.
  • It ensures variational problems involving entropy are convex.
  • It contrasts with optimal transport geometry, where entropy behaves differently (leading to displacement convexity).

There’s also a very deep connection between

negative entropy convexity \( ightarrow\) KL divergence convexity \( ightarrow\) exponential families \( ightarrow\) mirror descent

which is one of the most beautiful structures in information geometry. Later story.

5.2] Wasserstein Geometry: a Different Notion of Straight Line

Given the great properties above, however, many geometric functionals are not convex under mixture interpolation.

But hold on, you might ask, if mixture convexity holds for negative entropy, why bother studying other kinds of convexity?

This is an excellent and deep question. The short answer is: Mixture convexity lives in the wrong geometry for many physical and dynamical problems, whereas optimal transport reveals the correct geometry of probability space.

Importantly, mixture interpolation is not physical transport. Say we have

\[ ho_t = (1-t)p + tq\]

this literally means flip a coin with heads up with probability \(t\) and down with \((1-t)\). If it’s Head, then sample from \(q\) and sample from \(q\) otherwise. Since we just randomly choose distribution \(p\) or \(q\), it does not move mass. In extreme case, if \(p = \delta_{-5}, q = \delta_{5}\), then mixture interpolation gives us just two bumps, because both distributions coexist.

However, if we kind of expect the interpolation to be smearing/smoothing/averaging them together in some sense, which actually corresponds transport mass and just one bump moving from \(5\) to \(-5\).

This is what Wasserstein geodesics produce.

In optimal transport, probability measures are not treated as a linear space but as a Riemannian-like metric space (Otto’s theory, will elaborate in the following blogs)

\[(\mathcal{P}_2(\mathbb{R}^d), W_2)\]

where $W_2$ is the 2-Wasserstein distance.

In this geometry, the natural notion of a straight line is a Wasserstein geodesic.

Suppose the optimal transport map from \(\mu\) to \( u\) is

\[T(x).\]

Then the geodesic interpolation is

\[ ho_t = ig((1-t)I + tT ig)_{\#}\mu\]

This describes particles moving along straight lines from \(x\) to \(T(x)\).

From the above we shall see that

  • mixture interpolation = blending distributions
  • optimal transport interpolation = moving mass

5.3] Displacement Convexity

A functional \(F( ho)\) is called displacement convex if along the Wasserstein geodesic \( ho_t\)

\[F( ho_t) \le (1-t)F(\mu) + tF( u).\]

5.4] Entropy is Displacement Convex

Consider the differential entropy

\[H( ho)= \int ho(x)\log ho(x)\,dx.\]

(notice that here we’re not using the negative sign (you could add if you want, then just switch convex to concave, not a problem at all)) A fundamental theorem by Robert J. McCann (1997) shows:

The entropy functional is convex along Wasserstein geodesics.

So indeed

\[t \mapsto H[ ho_t]\]

is convex when \( ho_t\) is the optimal transport interpolation. This is called McCann displacement convexity.

This property implies:

  • uniqueness of gradient flows
  • stability of diffusion
  • functional inequalities
  • curvature bounds of spaces

Consequently, entropy behaves nicely in both convexities, but the displacement convexity describes real dynamics.

5.5] Why This Happens (Intuition)

The key reason is that optimal transport preserves the structure of mass movement.

Under optimal transport interpolation:

  • the density evolves according to a continuity equation
  • the Jacobian of the transport map controls density change.

Specifically,

\[ ho_t(x) = rac{ ho_0(y)} {\det ig((1-t)I + tDT(y) ig)}.\]

This determinant interacts with the $\log ho$ term in entropy in a way that produces convexity.

This phenomenon is deeply related to:

  • the Brunn–Minkowski inequality
  • convexity of the log-determinant function

6] Many PDEs Follow Displacement Geometry

Displacement convexity is extremely important because it implies gradient flows in Wasserstein space. Indeed, apart from entropy, many other important equations evolve along Wasserstein geometry, not mixture geometry. For example,

  • Fokker–Planck equation:
\[\partial_t ho = abla \cdot ( ho abla V) + \Delta ho\]

Jordan–Kinderlehrer–Otto (1998) showed:

This PDE is gradient descent of entropy in Wasserstein space.

Specifically,

\[\partial_t ho = abla \cdot ( ho abla \log ho) = \Delta ho\]

So entropy is not just concave along mixtures. It behaves as a convex energy along OT geodesics, and thus heat equation can be interpreted as the gradient flow of entropy in Wasserstein space.

7] Why This was a Big Discovery

Before optimal transport, probability space was treated as a linear space. Optimal transport revealed it actually has a Riemannian-like geometry. In that geometry:

  • geodesics = mass transport
  • entropy = convex energy
  • diffusion = gradient flow

This unified PDE, probability, geometry, and statistical physics.

8] Entropic Optimal Transport Flow

9]
entry.summary: Static and Dynamic Formulation of Optimal Transport Flow, Duality, Monge Problem

entry.title: The Flow of Time: Prob/Measure/Transport/Generative Mumble(3): A Tale of Two Perspectives

link.title: The Flow of Time: Prob/Measure/Transport/Generative Mumble(3): A Tale of Two Perspectives

entry.content:
1] Eulerian View

2] Lagragian View

3] An Important Lemma

3.1]

Why doest \(\Phi_t\) being a diffeomorphism lead to the fact that \(\det( abla \Phi_t) > 0\)?

Well, the key point is that being a diffeomorphism forces the Jacobian determinant to never vanish, but the positivity comes from continuity in time together with the initial condition. Let’s go step by step.

In the Lagrangian view of optimal transport we have a flow map

\[\partial_t \Phi_t(x) = V_t(\Phi_t(x)), \qquad \Phi_0(x) = x.\]

Here

  • \(\Phi_t : \mathbb{R}^d o \mathbb{R}^d\) is the flow map
  • \(V_t\) is a smooth velocity field.

So \(\Phi_t\) is generated by an ODE flow.

Then, if \(\Phi_t\) is a diffeomorphism, then

  • \(\Phi_t\) is smooth
  • \(\Phi_t^{-1}\) exists and is smooth.

By the inverse function theorem, this implies

\[\det( abla \Phi_t(x)) eq 0\]

for every \(x\). Otherwise the map would locally collapse dimension and would not be invertible. So diffeomorphism naturally leads to Jacobian determinant never vanishes.

Then Why is the determinant is positive? Now consider the initial condition \(\Phi_0(x) = x\), so

\[ abla \Phi_0(x) = I.\]

Thus

\[\det( abla \Phi_0(x)) = 1.\]

An important fact: The determinant evolves continuously in time because \(\Phi_t\) is smooth in \(t\)! If the determinant were ever to become negative, it would have to cross zero at some time. But

\[\det( abla \Phi_t(x)) = 0\]

would violate the diffeomorphism property. Therefore the determinant cannot change sign. Since it starts positive,

\[\det( abla \Phi_t(x)) > 0\]

for all \(t\).

3.2] Geometric interpretation

\(\det( abla \Phi_t)\) measures local volume distortion.

  • positive determinant → orientation preserved
  • negative determinant → orientation flipped

A smooth flow starting from the identity cannot suddenly flip orientation without collapsing volume first. But collapsing volume would break invertibility. So the flow preserves orientation.

3.3] Another way to see it (Jacobian ODE)

Define

\[J_t(x) = \det( abla \Phi_t(x)).\]

Then one can derive

\[ rac{d}{dt} J_t(x) = ( abla \cdot V_t)(\Phi_t(x))\, J_t(x).\]

Solving:

\[J_t(x) = \exp\!\left( \int_0^t ( abla\cdot V_s)(\Phi_s(x))\,ds ight).\]

Since the exponential is always positive,

\[J_t(x) > 0.\]

3.3] Final intuition

A diffeomorphic flow generated by a smooth velocity field:

  • starts from the identity
  • continuously deforms space
  • never tears or folds space

Therefore

\[\det( abla \Phi_t) > 0\]

because the map cannot cross zero Jacobian without losing invertibility.

4] Equivalence and Proof

5] Velocity Fields on Example Probability Flows

5.1] Ex.1 Linear Interpolation

Note: \(\Phi\) being a diffeomorphism does not necessitate that \(\Phi_t\) is also a diffeomorphism (might not be invertible)!

5.2] Overdamped Langevin Flow

5.3] Mixture Flow

5.4] Convolution FLow

\( ext{Law} ( ilde{\Phi}_t(X_0, X_1)) eq ext{Law}(\Phi_t(X))\). The left hand side is a fake flow (crossings could happen).

5.5] Heat Flow

Heat flow is also a convolution flow.

Two derivations of vector fields inform each other.

Association with the Tweedie’s formula, score, and Diffusion Model.

5.6] Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Flow

Just Langevin dynamics with Gaussian target; or convolution flow similar to the heat flow.

5.7] Poisson Flow
entry.summary: Eulerian and Lagrangian Interpretation, Vector Fields Optimal Transport

entry.title: The Flow of Time: Prob/Measure/Transport/Generative Mumble(2): Transport Maps and Couplings

link.title: The Flow of Time: Prob/Measure/Transport/Generative Mumble(2): Transport Maps and Couplings

entry.content:
1] Transport Map

1.1] Definition

1.2] Measure Preservation

1.3] Pushforward Sampling

1.4] Change of Variables Formula

1.5] Co-area Fomula

1.6] Transport Maps in 1 Dimension

Similaity to transition maps in manifolds between coordinate domains?

Optimal Transport maps are in some sense generalizations of this 1D transport map based on CDF’s.

1.7] Transport Maps in High Dimensions (Knothe-Rosenblatt map)

2] Coupling

2.1] Definition

From joint distribution to coupling.

Note taht this set is alwasy nonempty, because we could always take the product measure (\(X\) and \(Y\) are independent, if you may)

2.2] Examples

2.2.1] Deterministic vs. Product Coupling

Almost the opposite of product coupling and they are almost never the same, even in your setting where

\[ u = \Phi_{\#}\mu .\]

Let’s go through the structure carefully, first of all deterministic coupling induced by the map. Let

\[X \sim \mu, \qquad Y = \Phi(X).\]

Then the joint distribution of \((X,Y)\) is

\[\gamma = ( ext{Id},\phi)_{\#}\mu\]

Explicitly, for measurable sets \(A,B\):

\[\gamma(A imes B) = \mu ig(\{x\in A : \phi(x)\in B\} ig).\]

This coupling has marginals

  • first marginal: \(\mu\)
  • second marginal: \(\phi_{\#}\mu = u\)

so it is indeed a coupling between \(\mu\) and \( u\).

The key property for the above joint law is that the conditional distribution satisfies

\[Y|X=x \sim \delta_{\phi(x)}(y).\]

So \(Y\) is completely determined by \(X\). This is why it is called a deterministic coupling.

On the other hand, there always exist the produc coupling:

\[\gamma_{ ext{prod}} = \mu \otimes u.\]

For measurable sets:

\[\gamma_{ ext{prod}}(A imes B) = \mu(A) u(B).\]

Its conditional distribution is

\[Y|X=x \sim u.\]

So here

  • \(X\) and \(Y\) are independent.

The deterministic coupling and the product coupling are different. The deterministic coupling lives on the graph of \(\phi\):

\[\{(x,\phi(x))\}.\]

whereas the product coupling spreads mass over the entire product space.

Well, this actually has some practical advantages for transport map instead of coupling (transport plan) in terms of parameter storage, and in fact quite often we will be able to find a transport map in optimal transport problem, which is indeed in most cases preferrable. We will come back to this later.

2.2.2] An Example

Let

\[X \sim ext{Uniform}[0,1], \qquad \Phi(x)=x.\]

Then

\[ u = \mu.\]

For the deterministic coupling, the joint law is

\[(X,X)\]

Support: \(\{(x,x)\}\), which is just the diagonal line.

On the other hand, for the product coupling, the joint law stays on

\[(X,Y), \quad X,Y ext{ i.i.d. Uniform}[0,1]\]

where the support is \([0,1]^2\), which is the whole square.

These are clearly different distributions.

2.2.3] When Would They Coincide?

They coincide only in degenerate cases. For example if \( u\) is a Dirac measure

\[ u = \delta_{y_0}\]

and

\[\Phi(x)=y_0.\]

Then

  • deterministic coupling: $(X,y_0)$
  • product coupling: $(X,Y)$ with $Y=y_0$ a.s.

So they match in this specific case, but this is exceptional.

What I want to emphasize is that knowing that

\[ u = \Phi_{\#}\mu\]

does not restrict couplings to deterministic ones.

It only tells us one particular coupling exists:

\[( ext{Id},\Phi)_{\#}\mu .\]

But the set of couplings

\[\Pi(\mu, u)\]

is huge — it includes

  • deterministic couplings
  • product coupling
  • infinitely many others.

Later in optimal transport, we will observe that the deterministic coupling

\[( ext{Id},\phi)_{\#}\mu\]

is called a transport map, but most couplings are transport plans that cannot be written as maps.

In summary, if we indeed have a map \(Y=\Phi(X)\), then we would naturally obtain a deterministic coupling \(\gamma = ( ext{Id},\phi)_{\#}\mu\)

while in most cases it’s different from product coupling, which always exists between two measures

\[\gamma = \mu\otimes u\]

\(X\) and \(Y\) independent.

They are almost never the same.

3] Clarifications and Differences

It’s not always possible to establish a transport map from \(\mu\) to \( u\).

For example, the Kronecker Delta.

However, apart from this, in general is there a transport map?

If we could then find out a transport map between \(\mu\) to \( u\), then does that mean that there exists a deterministic coupling among others?

4] A Broader Geometric Picture

It’s hard to conceal the deeper truth behind such differences and connections between deterministic vs product coupling. However, readers not familiar with optimal transport (I will cover that in the not too distance future) feel free to skip this and come back later. Specifically, here I would like to clarify how deterministic couplings sit inside the full coupling polytope and why optimal transport often selects them. The following applied to all couplings when $ u = \Phi_{#}\mu$

Suppose

\[ u = \Phi_{\#}\mu\]

for some measurable map \(\Phi : X o Y\), or we could just say \(Y = \Phi(X), \qquad X \sim \mu\).

This means

\[\gamma(X, Y) = \mu(X)\delta_{\Phi(X)}(Y)\]

is one possible coupling, or one transport plan between \(\mu\) and \( u\).

However, from the above discussions we should already be intuitive and comfortable with the idea that the space of all couplings between \(\mu\) and \( u\) is much larger. Consequently, understanding this space geometrically helps clarify the role of deterministic transport maps.

4.1] The Space of All Couplings

The set of couplings is

\[\Pi(\mu, u) = \{\gamma \in \mathcal{P}(X imes Y) : (\pi_X)_{\#}\gamma = \mu,\; (\pi_Y)_{\#}\gamma = u \}.\]

So every coupling

  • spreads mass over the product space \(X imes Y\)
  • while preserving the two marginals.

Geometrically:

  • \(\Pi(\mu, u)\) is a convex set (I’ll let readers to prove this claim)
  • Mixtures of couplings remain couplings.

Indeed, if

\[\gamma_1,\gamma_2 \in \Pi(\mu, u)\]

then

\[\gamma_t = t\gamma_1 + (1-t)\gamma_2, \,\, t \in [0, 1]\]

is also a valid coupling (this is almost obvious).

So $\Pi(\mu, u)$ behaves like a convex polytope (or convex body) in the space of measures.

4.2] Deterministic Couplings = Graphs

The deterministic coupling induced by $\Phi$ is

\[\gamma_{\Phi} = (\mathrm{Id},\Phi)_{\#}\mu.\]

Its support is

\[ ext{supp}(\gamma_{\Phi}) = \{(x,\Phi(x))\}.\]

which is the graph of \(\Phi\).

So geometrically, all mass lies on a lower-dimensional surface, instead of filling the whole product space.

For example (the same as above), if

\[X=Y=[0,1], \quad \Phi(x)=x\]

then

  • deterministic coupling → diagonal line
  • product coupling → full square

4.3] Discrete Case: The Transportation Polytope

The geometry becomes very clear in the finite case.

Suppose

\[\mu = (a_1,\dots,a_n), \qquad u = (b_1,\dots,b_m).\]

A coupling is a matrix

\[\Gamma = (\gamma_{ij})\]

satisfying

\[\sum_j \gamma_{ij} = a_i\] \[\sum_i \gamma_{ij} = b_j\] \[\gamma_{ij} \ge 0\]

This set forms a transportation polytope. Perhaps it’s a little more intuitive to see that geometrically it’s a convex polytope in \(\mathbb{R}^{nm}\).

Consequently, for this problem the vertices of this polytope correspond to deterministic transport plans.

In particular

  • extreme points have at most \(n+m-1\) nonzero entries
  • many correspond to permutation-like assignments.

These are the most structured couplings.

Another perspective is to interpret this as a linear programming optimization problem, which I will elaborate more later when actually go into optimal transport!

4.4] Where the Deterministic Map Lives

When \( u = \Phi_{\#}\mu\), the coupling \(\gamma_\Phi\) lies inside $\Pi(\mu, u)$. More precisely, it sits on the boundary of this convex set. Why?

Because it concentrates mass on the graph constraint

\[y=\Phi(x).\]

This is a very low-entropy configuration. In contrast:

  • product coupling spreads mass everywhere
  • mixtures smear the distribution across the square.

4.5] Why Optimal Transport Selects Deterministic Maps

Optimal transport (Kantorovich problem) solves

\[\min_{\gamma\in\Pi(\mu, u)} \int c(x,y)\, d\gamma(x,y).\]

But notice that this optimization problem is

A linear objective over a convex set

Since

  • \(\Pi(\mu, u)\) is convex
  • It’s linear in \(\gamma\)
  • deterministic plans correspond to extreme points

the optimizer often lands on a deterministic transport map.

4.6] Brenier’s Theorem (quadratic cost)

If

  • \(\mu\) is absolutely continuous
  • the cost is
\[c(x,y)=|x-y|^2\]

then the optimal coupling is

\[\gamma = (\mathrm{Id},T)_{\#}\mu\]

for a map

\[T = abla \psi\]

where $\psi$ is convex. Notice that the Brenier’s Theorem is special not because it exposes that \(T\) exists and is a gradient (which has been known to many even before Brenier for a long time), but that it’s a gradient of a convex function. Conseuqently, the optimal plan collapses onto a deterministic graph.

4.7] Entropy Perspective

Deterministic coupling corresponds to minimum entropy, while product coupling indicates maximum entropy. Later on we will discuss one practical alrogithm (Sinkhorn) for solving the optimal transport optimizaton problem, on a slightly regugalirzed form of this problem called Entropic Optimal Transport, which intentionally pushes solutions away from deterministic vertices toward the interior of the polytope.

4.8] Intuitive Summary

Key ideas:

  • deterministic couplings sit on the boundary/extreme points
  • mixtures fill the interior
  • optimal transport prefers extreme points, which explains why transport maps appear naturally

Even if \( u=\Phi_{\#}\mu\), the deterministic coupling is just one corner of a huge convex set of possible couplings — optimal transport often picks that corner because linear costs prefer extreme points of the coupling polytope.

entry.title: The Flow of Time: Prob/Measure/Transport/Generative Mumble(1): Probability Flows

link.title: The Flow of Time: Prob/Measure/Transport/Generative Mumble(1): Probability Flows

entry.content:
This is the starting blog of a new series. Distinct from the Geometry Mumble series, this collection of blogs will primarily deal with the other side of my interest: the temporal effects along sampling.

0] High Dimensional Sampling

Sampling from a complex, arbitrary, high-dimensional distribution—like trying to find a needle in a haystack where the haystack is the size of the solar system—is one of the most significant hurdles in modern statistics and machine learning. When we say “arbitrary,” we mean the distribution doesn’t follow a nice, predictable shape like a Gaussian (Bell Curve). When we say “high-dimensional,” we mean we are dealing with hundreds, thousands, or even millions of variables simultaneously.

0.1] The Curse of Dimensionality

As the number of dimensions (\(d\)) increases, the “volume” of the space grows exponentially. If we want to maintain the same density of sample points in 100-dimensional space as we have in 2-dimensional space, we would need more samples than there are atoms in the observable universe.

  • Concentration of Measure: In high dimensions, most of the “mass” of a distribution lives in a very thin “shell” or manifold far away from the mode (the peak) (check this blogs and this blog).
  • The Search Problem: Most of the space is “empty” (near-zero probability). A random sampler will spend almost all its time exploring regions that contribute nothing to the overall understanding of the distribution.

0.2] The Normalization Constant (The Partition Function)

Most complex distributions are defined by a function \(f(x)\), but to make it a true probability distribution, it must integrate to 1. This requires dividing by a normalization constant \(Z\):

\[P(x) = rac{f(x)}{Z} ext{ where } Z = \int f(x) dx\]

In high dimensions, calculating this integral is computationally intractable. While algorithms like Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) allow us to sample without knowing \(Z\), they face significant efficiency bottlenecks.

(> Note: Modern techniques like Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) attempt to solve these issues by using the gradient of the distribution to “glide” along the geometry, much like a puck sliding on a frictionless surface).

0.3] Multimodality and “Islands”

A complex distribution often has multiple “modes” (peaks) separated by vast valleys of low probability.

  • Trapping: A sampler might get stuck in one peak, unaware that a much larger peak exists elsewhere.
  • Mixing Time: The time it takes for a sampler to “forget” its starting position and move between these islands grows exponentially with the height and width of the barriers between them.

0.4] Anisotropy and Local Geometry

In high dimensions, the distribution might be very “stretched” in some directions and “compressed” in others.

  • Step Size Dilemma: If your sampler takes steps that are too large, it overshoots the narrow regions and gets rejected. If the steps are too small, it takes forever to explore the long, stretched dimensions.
  • Correlation: When variables are highly correlated, the “path” the sampler must follow is a narrow, winding ridge in a massive space.

1] Probability Flows

Flows of probability measures underly a variety of techniques and theories, ranging from generative modeling like Diffusion and Flow based models, or more classical domains of math like optimal transport. Many seemingly intuitive formulations carry with themselves deep fundamental explanations. This blog will start from a very basic survey of options of changing, the different probability flows.
